context: The Passover under Josiah and the account of his death (vv. 26-31). Josiah was the last king of Judah to seek truth (v. 21). As such, he is a figure of Christ, who is the embodiment of truth ([[JOH.14:6]]). From Exodus 12 we learn that the Feast of the Passover is an OT type of the NT paschal feast ([[1CO.10:23]]-26), in which the Church celebrates the death and Resurrection of Christ.

The Passover under Josiah and the account of his death (vv. 26-31). Josiah was the last king of Judah to seek truth (v. 21). As such, he is a figure of Christ, who is the embodiment of truth ([[JOH.14:6]]). From Exodus 12 we learn that the Feast of the Passover is an OT type of the NT paschal feast ([[1CO.10:23]]-26), in which the Church celebrates the death and Resurrection of Christ.

Vineyards and wine (v. 16) symbolize the Eucharist, and clothing (v. 17) points to being clothed with Christ in baptism. As woman is the glory of man ([[1CO.11:7]]), so the Church is the glory of Christ ([[EPH.5:21]]).

This passage chronicles the completion of the temple under the leadership of Haggai and Zechariah. The building of the earthly temple is a type of our deification-- becoming like God by His grace ([[1CO.6:19]]).
Ask what you will: As the Father gives all things to the Son, so Christ gives all things to those who ask according to His will ([[MAT.21:22]]; [[JOH.14:14]]; [[1JN.5:15]]).

Purple denotes royalty and equality with the king (see note on [[EST.6:6]]-9); the gold cups symbolize Christ's cup of salvation; sleep in a gold bed foreshadows His rest in the tomb; the chariot--like Elijah's chariot of fire (3Kg 2:11)--foreshadows the Ascension; the linen head covering points both to the OT priesthood and to the linen in which Christ's body was wrapped ([[JOH.20:7]]); and the necklace symbolizes virtue (Pr 3:3, 22; 6:21; SS 1:10; see also [[DAN.5:7]]).

The strength of wine symbolizes the "new wine" of the new covenant ([[MAT.9:15]]- 17): the Holy Spirit uniting with and dwelling within men. St. Paul writes, "And do not be drunk with wine... but be filled with the Spirit" ([[EPH.5:18]]).

Vineyards and wine (v. 16) symbolize the Eucharist, and clothing (v. 17) points to being clothed with Christ in baptism. As woman is the glory of man ([[1CO.11:7]]), so the Church is the glory of Christ ([[EPH.5:21]]).

Women prevail, for women fulfill man's purpose. God intended for men and women to be united in love, just as Christ and His Church are united in love ([[EPH.5:22]], [[EPH.5:23]]). Thus, as Zerubbabel describes women, we see Christ describing His relationship with His Bride.

Women prevail, for women fulfill man's purpose. God intended for men and women to be united in love, just as Christ and His Church are united in love ([[EPH.5:22]], [[EPH.5:23]]). Thus, as Zerubbabel describes women, we see Christ describing His relationship with His Bride.
This is a paraphrase of [[GEN.2:24]]. According to St. Paul, the union of man and wife in marriage is an image of Christ and His Church ([[EPH.5:30]]-32; see note at 4:14, 15).

Purple denotes royalty and equality with the king (see note on [[EST.6:6]]-9); the gold cups symbolize Christ's cup of salvation; sleep in a gold bed foreshadows His rest in the tomb; the chariot--like Elijah's chariot of fire (3Kg 2:11)--foreshadows the Ascension; the linen head covering points both to the OT priesthood and to the linen in which Christ's body was wrapped ([[JOH.20:7]]); and the necklace symbolizes virtue (Pr 3:3, 22; 6:21; SS 1:10; see also [[DAN.5:7]]).

These three young men who speak as one are, like the three men at the trees of Mamre ([[GEN.18:2]]), another OT symbol of the Holy Trinity. Their role as the king's bodyguards illustrates the way in which the unknowable, inconceivable God reveals Himself in His creation as Triune.

The concubine is often an OT figure of fallen humanity. She has received God's crown, that is His divine image and likeness, on her own head in creation ([[GEN.1:26]]). On the other hand, she has struck the king--indeed, killed him--that she might be reconciled with him, that is, with Christ.
This is a paraphrase of [[GEN.2:24]]. According to St. Paul, the union of man and wife in marriage is an image of Christ and His Church ([[EPH.5:30]]-32; see note at 4:14, 15).

The request of Zerubbabel, echoed in the prayer of the repentant thief (Lk 23:42), reveals the will of God for the world: rebuild Jerusalem, that is, "the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem" ([[HEB.12:22]]). Send back all the vessels (v. 44) and rebuild the temple (v. 45) in a spiritual sense means to restore holiness in the hearts of men, which have been laid waste by sin.
Ask what you will: As the Father gives all things to the Son, so Christ gives all things to those who ask according to His will ([[MAT.21:22]]; [[JOH.14:14]]; [[1JN.5:15]]).

The Passover under Josiah and the account of his death (vv. 26-31). Josiah was the last king of Judah to seek truth (v. 21). As such, he is a figure of Christ, who is the embodiment of truth ([[JOH.14:6]]). From Exodus 12 we learn that the Feast of the Passover is an OT type of the NT paschal feast ([[1CO.10:23]]-26), in which the Church celebrates the death and Resurrection of Christ.

Purple denotes royalty and equality with the king (see note on [[EST.6:6]]-9); the gold cups symbolize Christ's cup of salvation; sleep in a gold bed foreshadows His rest in the tomb; the chariot--like Elijah's chariot of fire (3Kg 2:11)--foreshadows the Ascension; the linen head covering points both to the OT priesthood and to the linen in which Christ's body was wrapped ([[JOH.20:7]]); and the necklace symbolizes virtue (Pr 3:3, 22; 6:21; SS 1:10; see also [[DAN.5:7]]).

That the hearers of the law were inspired by the words of the Scriptures brings to mind the response of the disciples ("Did not our heart burn within us?") as the Lord Jesus Christ opened to them the Holy Scriptures ([[LUK.24:32]]).
The third, named Zerubbabel, is the only one of the three bodyguards who is named. He is of Christ's lineage ([[MAT.1:12]]), a leader in the rebuilding of the temple, and a figure of Christ.
Ask what you will: As the Father gives all things to the Son, so Christ gives all things to those who ask according to His will ([[MAT.21:22]]; [[JOH.14:14]]; [[1JN.5:15]]).

The strength of wine symbolizes the "new wine" of the new covenant ([[MAT.9:15]]- 17): the Holy Spirit uniting with and dwelling within men. St. Paul writes, "And do not be drunk with wine... but be filled with the Spirit" ([[EPH.5:18]]).

, 2 This great banquet is also an icon of creation, which is itself a symbol of Christ's work in His Incarnation. The Feast of Passover described in 1 Ezra also images the heavenly banquet ([[REV.19:7]]-9), and both are experienced today in the Holy Eucharist. As the psalmist writes, "But God is our King before the ages; / He worked salvation in the midst of the earth" ([[PSA.73:12]]).
